verses.text: It came to pass a long time after that the LORD had given rest unto Israel from all their enemies round about, that Joshua waxed old and stricken in age. Joshua called for all Israel, and for their elders, and for their heads, and for their judges, and for their officers, and said unto them: I am old and stricken in age. Guidance: 'A long time' — perhaps a generation since the conquest ended. Rest has settled. Joshua, who in chapter 13 was already called 'old,' is now older. He convenes the entire leadership council: elders, heads, judges, officers. A farewell address is coming. The first word is honest about himself before it presses anything on others: 'I am old.' Leaders who admit their mortality have credibility when they speak about everyone's future.

Ye have seen all that the LORD your God hath done unto all these nations because of you; for the LORD your God is he that hath fought for you. Behold, I have divided unto you by lot these nations that remain, to be an inheritance for your tribes, from Jordan, with all the nations that I have cut off, even unto the great sea westward. The LORD your God, he shall expel them from before you, and drive them from out of your sight; and ye shall possess their land, as the LORD your God hath promised unto you. Guidance: Joshua does not begin with what is left to do; he begins with what has been done. 'The LORD your God is he that hath fought for you.' Every victory the elders have seen with their own eyes credited to the right account. Then he hands off the rest: God will drive out the nations that remain. The retiring leader leaves the people with a promise that does not depend on him.

Be ye therefore very courageous to keep and to do all that is written in the book of the law of Moses, that ye turn not aside therefrom to the right hand or to the left; that ye come not among these nations, these that remain among you; neither make mention of the name of their gods, nor cause to swear by them, neither serve them, nor bow yourselves unto them: but cleave unto the LORD your God, as ye have done unto this day. Guidance: 'Be very courageous' — same phrase God spoke to Joshua at the start of the book (1:7), now passed to the elders. But courage for what? Not for battle this time; for not naming. 'Make no mention of their gods.' Words carry idols. Sometimes spiritual warfare is verbal hygiene. And the positive: 'cleave unto the LORD' — the same Hebrew verb used for marriage in Gen 2:24. The relationship that began as conquest must continue as covenant intimacy.

The LORD hath driven out from before you great nations and strong: but as for you, no man hath been able to stand before you unto this day. One man of you shall chase a thousand: for the LORD your God, he it is that fighteth for you, as he hath promised you. Take good heed therefore unto yourselves, that ye love the LORD your God. Guidance: 'One man of you shall chase a thousand' — a math problem that only the LORD can solve. The leveling of armies is not the leveling of human strength but the multiplication of divine help. Then the takeaway: 'Take good heed unto yourselves, that ye love the LORD.' Loving is not optional sentiment; it is the active condition of victory. The same chapter that promised supernatural odds requires personal affection. The ratio is gospel; the discipline is love.

Else if ye do in any wise go back, and cleave unto the remnant of these nations, even these that remain among you, and shall make marriages with them, and go in unto them, and they to you: know for a certainty that the LORD your God will no more drive out any of these nations from before you; but they shall be snares and traps unto you, and scourges in your sides, and thorns in your eyes, until ye perish from off this good land which the LORD your God hath given you. Guidance: Four images stacked: snares (hidden tripwires for animals), traps (bait that hides a hook), scourges (whips at your ribs), thorns (irritants in the eye). All four describe what compromise feels like over time: not catastrophe at once, but a slow, painful, blinding accumulation. Marriage is named directly — the most intimate point of cultural transfer. Solomon will fall by exactly this in 1 Kings 11. The chapter that wins by cleaving to the LORD loses by cleaving to anyone else.

Behold, this day I am going the way of all the earth: ye know in all your hearts and in all your souls, that not one thing hath failed of all the good things which the LORD your God spake concerning you; all are come to pass unto you, and not one thing hath failed thereof. Therefore it shall come to pass, that as all good things are come upon you, which the LORD your God promised you; so shall the LORD bring upon you all evil things, until he have destroyed you from off this good land which the LORD your God hath given you. When ye have transgressed the covenant of the LORD your God, which he commanded you, and have gone and served other gods, and bowed yourselves to them; then shall the anger of the LORD be kindled against you, and ye shall perish quickly from off the good land which he hath given unto you. Guidance: 'I am going the way of all the earth' — a calm dying man's vocabulary. Same God, two outcomes. The argument is symmetrical, almost shocking: if the LORD's faithfulness to His promises is as visible as you have seen, then His faithfulness to His warnings will be equally visible. Israel's history will prove him right — Judges, the divided kingdom, exile. Joshua does not soften the second half because he loved them. Real farewell sermons name both pulses of the covenant: come close, and stay close.
